summarization.a: The chart shows the layer density and composition of GeSe<sub>(1-x)</sub> as a function of cycle number for SiO<sub>2</sub>, TiN, and SiO<sub>2</sub>, DFM.
summarization.b: The chart illustrates the growth rate and composition of GeSe<sub>(1-x)</sub> as a function of substrate temperature for SiO<sub>2</sub> and TiN.

data_extraction.b:
| Substrate Temperature (°C) | Growth rate (ngcm<sup>-2</sup>cy<sup>-1</sup>) | x in GeSe<sub>(1-x)</sub> |
|---|---|---|
| 70 | 57 | 0.5 |
| 90 | 26 | 0.5 |
| 110 | 19 | 0.5 |
| 130 | 10 | 0.6 |

a.question: What is an expected trend for perfect ALD with respect to layer density and cycle number?
a.answer: a	For perfect ALD, every cycle, the amount of deposited material is the same, meaning there should be a linear relation between the cycle number and the layer density.

a.question: Can different dosing schemes be used to differ the material composition?
a.answer: a	For all substrates and dosing methods the composition is the same, i.e. Ge:Se is 1:1. This means that differently dosing the precursors will not result in different composition.

b.question: What could be the reason for decreasing growth rate for increasing temperature?
b.answer: b	This can be caused by desorption of adsorbed species leading to less film growth.

b.question: Which precursor dose is more susceptible to desorption upon increasing temperature?
b.answer: b	With increasing temperature the Ge contribution increases in the film, meaning there is less Se. This means that the Se precursor desorbs more easily.
